title: The Last Text I Never Sent
story: I typed out the message three times before deleting it. 'I miss you, Dad.' It had been two years since he passed, and I still couldn't bring myself to delete his number. Every birthday, every holiday, I'd open our chat and just stare at his last message: 'Drive safe, kiddo. Love you.' One night, I finally hit send. The message went through. Then three dots appeared. My heart stopped. A reply came back: 'This is his old number. But I want you to know — he loved you. He talked about you all the time. I'm his nurse from the hospital. I kept this phone because I knew someone might need to hear that someday.' I cried for an hour. Sometimes the universe sends you exactly what you need.

title: The Empty Chair at Graduation
story: There was an empty chair in the front row at my graduation. My mom had saved it with a photo of my dad and a single red rose. He'd died when I was fifteen. Cancer. He once told me, 'I might not make it to your graduation, but I'll be there.' When they called my name, I walked across the stage, diploma in hand, and looked at that chair. The photo was face-down. The rose had wilted. But sitting in the chair behind it was a man in a janitor's uniform, crying. After the ceremony, he found me and said, 'Your dad and I were in the same hospital room. He made me promise I'd come.' I held a stranger and sobbed.

title: She Waited at the Bus Stop Every Day
story: REPLACE THIS WITH YOUR OWN STORY SCRIPT. Each story should be roughly 60-90 seconds when read aloud (approximately 150-250 words). Write in a first-person, emotional, storytelling style that hooks viewers in the first sentence.
